ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for Mala Xiang Guo exists; mapped to the closest parent class 'stir-fried food product' (FOODON_03315595) as a defensible approximate anchor.
ontology.foodex2_description: Composite dishes — stir-fried mixed meat and vegetables
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Mala Xiang Guo; A0FJP (composite stir-fried mixed dish) is the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for Mala Xiang Guo or its parent category in the clinical terminology hierarchy.

main_ingredients: mala paste (fermented broad beans, Szechuan peppercorns, dried chilies, chili oil)

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard restaurant serving of Mala Xiang Guo (dry) is approximately 400 g, representing a single-person portion of the stir-fried mixed dish as typically served in Singaporean and Chinese eateries.

nutrient_source.title: Energy and Nutrient Composition of Food — Singapore HPB Foodo Database; supplemented by USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for composite stir-fried mixed dish components
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board (HPB) Singapore; United States Department of Agriculture (USDA)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single HPB Foodo entry exists for Mala Xiang Guo (dry) as a standalone record. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Foodo composite stir-fried mixed dish entries and cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for individual components (mixed meats, vegetables, chili oil, fermented bean paste). The input record's per-100g values (energy 185 kcal, protein 8.2 g, fat 14.5 g, carbohydrate 6.8 g, sodium 450 mg) are consistent with this adapted composite and are retained. Saturated fat estimated at approximately 4.5 g per 100 g based on typical chili oil and animal fat contribution in Szechuan stir-fry preparations per USDA SR Legacy data for comparable dishes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — analogous estimate based on mixed stir-fried vegetable and meat dish with high fat and fibre content
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Mala Xiang Guo (dry) in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 42 (low) is an analogous estimate based on: (1) comparable mixed stir-fried vegetable and meat dishes with high fat, protein, and fibre content in the Sydney GI Database (GI range 35–50); (2) the fat-attenuation effect of significant chili oil and animal fat content slowing gastric emptying; (3) the fibre contribution from mixed vegetables and mushrooms. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (42 × 27.2) / 100 = 11.4. Carbohydrate per 400 g serving = 6.8 × 4 = 27.2 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium content (450 mg per 100 g; 1800 mg per 400 g serving) from mala paste, fermented broad beans, and seasoning sauces
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid Mala Xiang Guo due to its very high sodium content of approximately 1800 mg per standard serving, which represents approximately 78–90% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000–2300 mg; if consumed, request reduced paste and sauce, and avoid adding extra salt or soy sauce.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (estimated GI 42) and moderate carbohydrate content (6.8 g per 100 g), but high fat content (14.5 g per 100 g) and high sodium may complicate overall metabolic management
type2_diabetes.guidance: Mala Xiang Guo (dry) has a low estimated glycemic index and relatively low carbohydrate content, making it a moderate-concern option for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without rice or noodles; however, the high fat content (particularly saturated fat from chili oil and animal fat) and very high sodium should be monitored, and potato-heavy ingredient selections should be avoided to prevent glycemic spikes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (14.5 g per 100 g) with estimated saturated fat of 4.5 g per 100 g from chili oil and animal fats, combined with very high sodium (450 mg per 100 g), presents compounded cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume Mala Xiang Guo (dry) only occasionally and in small portions, given its high saturated fat content (estimated 4.5 g per 100 g) and very high sodium (1800 mg per 400 g serving), both of which are established risk factors for elevated LDL cholesterol and blood pressure; choosing leaner protein options (tofu, chicken) and requesting reduced oil and paste can partially mitigate risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Capsaicin from dried chilies and piperine from Szechuan peppercorns may have anti-inflammatory properties, but high oil and sodium content can be pro-inflammatory if consumed excessively
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Mala Xiang Guo may offer modest anti-inflammatory benefit from capsaicin and piperine in moderate amounts, but individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should limit portion size to avoid the pro-inflammatory effects of excessive saturated fat and sodium intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mala Xiang Guo (dry) (麻辣香锅) is a highly popular Szechuan-style dry stir-fry dish originating from Sichuan Province, China, and now widely consumed across East and Southeast Asia, including Singapore. The dish is defined by its signature 'mala' flavour profile — 'ma' (麻, numbing) derived from Szechuan peppercorns and 'la' (辣, spicy) from dried chilies and chili oil — creating an intensely aromatic, oily, and flavourful eating experience. Unlike its soupy counterpart (mala tang), the dry version is wok-fried at high heat until most liquid evaporates, resulting in a concentrated, fragrant coating on all ingredients. Diners typically select a customisable combination of raw ingredients including thinly sliced meats (beef, pork, chicken), seafood, tofu products, mushrooms, and vegetables such as lotus root, broccoli, cabbage, and potato, which are then flash-fried in a wok with a potent mala paste made from fermented broad beans (doubanjiang), ginger, garlic, Szechuan peppercorns, dried chilies, and chili oil. The texture is varied — tender meats, slightly crisp vegetables, and silky tofu — all coated in a rich, spicy, numbing sauce. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides approximately 185 kcal, 8.2 g protein, 14.5 g fat (including an estimated 4.5 g saturated fat), and 6.8 g carbohydrate, with a notably high sodium content of 450 mg. The dish has a low estimated glycemic index of approximately 42 due to its high fat, protein, and fibre content from mixed vegetables and mushrooms. Clinically, the very high sodium content (approximately 1800 mg per 400 g serving) is the primary concern for individuals with hypertension and cardiovascular disease. The high fat content, particularly saturated fat from chili oil and animal fats, also warrants caution for cardiovascular health. For individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, the low GI and moderate carbohydrate content are favourable, but the dish should be consumed without rice or noodles and with potato-heavy selections avoided. The dish is typically served as a main course at lunch or dinner, often in social group dining settings.

confidence_reason: No single approved source (HPB Foodo, USDA FoodData Central) contains an exact entry for Mala Xiang Guo (dry) as a standalone dish. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Foodo composite stir-fried mixed dish entries and cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component data. The input record's core macros are consistent with this adapted composite and are retained. Saturated fat is estimated. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database category for comparable mixed stir-fried dishes. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. Saturated fat estimated at 4.5 g per 100 g based on typical chili oil and animal fat contribution in Szechuan stir-fry preparations per USDA SR Legacy data for comparable dishes; no direct measurement available. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation, cholesterol, and moisture data for this customisable composite dish are not available in HPB Foodo or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. 3. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0: confirmed negligible/zero vitamin D in this dish type per USDA FoodData Central data for comparable stir-fried mixed meat and vegetable dishes; no significant vitamin D source is present in the standard ingredient set. 4. GI of 42 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Mala Xiang Guo (dry) exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 5. Halal status is not confirmed as the dish may contain pork depending on ingredient selection; is_halal is set to false as a conservative default. 6. The dish is highly customisable; nutrient values represent a typical mixed preparation and will vary significantly based on ingredient selection.
